title: Metformin in type 2 diabetes
text: Metformin is a first line oral medication for type 2 diabetes. It lowers blood glucose mainly by reducing glucose production in the liver and improving insulin sensitivity in peripheral tissues. It is usually weight neutral and carries a low risk of hypoglycaemia when used alone. A rare but serious side effect is lactic acidosis, so it is avoided in severe kidney impairment. Common side effects are gastrointestinal, including nausea and diarrhoea, which often improve with a slow dose increase.

title: ACE inhibitors and blood pressure
text: Angiotensin converting enzyme inhibitors lower blood pressure by blocking the conversion of angiotensin one to angiotensin two, a potent vasoconstrictor. They are widely used in hypertension and heart failure and protect kidney function in people with diabetes. A characteristic side effect is a dry persistent cough, caused by a build up of bradykinin. They should not be used in pregnancy because of the risk of harm to the fetus.

title: CRISPR Cas9 genome editing
text: CRISPR Cas9 is a tool for editing DNA at specific locations. A guide RNA directs the Cas9 enzyme to a matching DNA sequence, where it makes a cut. The cell then repairs the break, and this repair can be used to disable a gene or insert a new sequence. The technology is used in research, drug discovery and the development of gene therapies. A key safety concern is off target editing, where cuts occur at unintended sites.

title: Statins and cholesterol
text: Statins lower cholesterol by inhibiting HMG CoA reductase, an enzyme the liver uses to make cholesterol. They reduce low density lipoprotein cholesterol and lower the risk of heart attack and stroke. The most common side effect is muscle pain, and a rare but serious one is rhabdomyolysis, a breakdown of muscle tissue. Liver enzymes are sometimes monitored during treatment.

title: Antibiotic resistance
text: Antibiotic resistance happens when bacteria evolve so that drugs that once killed them no longer work. It is driven by overuse and misuse of antibiotics in people and animals. Resistant infections are harder to treat and increase the risk of severe illness. Strategies to slow resistance include using antibiotics only when needed, completing prescribed courses, and developing new classes of drugs.

title: mRNA vaccines
text: mRNA vaccines deliver a piece of messenger RNA that instructs cells to make a harmless fragment of a pathogen, such as a viral spike protein. The immune system recognises this fragment and learns to respond, so it can react quickly to a real infection later. The mRNA does not enter the cell nucleus and does not change a person's DNA. These vaccines can be designed and updated quickly, which is useful against evolving viruses.

title: Insulin and glucose regulation
text: Insulin is a hormone made by the beta cells of the pancreas. It allows cells to take up glucose from the blood for energy and signals the liver to store glucose as glycogen. In type 1 diabetes the body makes little or no insulin, so it must be replaced by injection. In type 2 diabetes the body becomes resistant to insulin over time. Too much insulin relative to food intake can cause low blood sugar.

title: How chemotherapy works
text: Chemotherapy uses drugs that target rapidly dividing cells, a hallmark of cancer. Because some healthy cells also divide quickly, such as those in hair follicles, bone marrow and the gut lining, chemotherapy often causes hair loss, low blood counts and nausea. Treatment is usually given in cycles to let healthy tissue recover between doses. Drugs are often combined to attack cancer cells in different ways.
